content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

March 18, 1996

Mr. Ross Henry
8124 Mountain Avenue
Orangevale, California 95662

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-058

Dear Mr. Henry:

This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1

QUESTIONS

Although you are retired now, because you were Chief of the
Planning, Acquisition and Local Services Division of the
California Department of Parks and Recreation, will there be a
potential conflict of interest if you sell your home and its
adjacent property to the county? Would the Act's revolving door
provisions affect the sale of your home to the county?

CONCLUSIONS

The sale of your home to the county would not create a
conflict of interest for you under the Act. The Act's conflicts
provisions apply only to public officials who are making
governmental decisions, not to individuals who have retired from
public service. The revolving door provisions of the Act would
not affect this transaction.

FACTS

On December 30, 1994, you retired from the California
Department of Parks and Recreation. The position you held prior
to retirement was Chief of the Planning, Acquisition and Local

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18995.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-96-058
Page 2


Services Division. Now that you have retired, you and your wife
have decided to move permanently to your home in Graeagle,
California, and to sell your home in Orangevale, which is adjacent
to Indian Stone Corral County Park. Gene Andal, Director of the
Sacramento County Department of Regional Parks and Recreation, has
indicated that the county is interested in purchasing your
Orangevale home and the adjacent property.

Your property in Orangevale is eight acres in size and is
contiguous to the county park along the eastern boundary for
approximately 500 feet. Linda Creek, which runs through the
county park, runs over 600 lineal feet through your property
before entering the park. The county has had an appraisal done on
the property, but you have not seen the appraisal. The county may
soon be making an offer on the property.

In your discussions with Gene Andal, he has indicated that
they intend to use funds from the 1988 Bond Act for the
acquisition. The 1988 Bond Act provided the county with over
six million dollars for discretionary park projects. The county
set aside a portion of these funds for an addition to Indian Stone
Corral County Park. Originally, the county intended to purchase
property on the west side of the park, but they were unable to
come to terms with the owner of that property. Now that your
property is available on the east side of the park, the county
intends to use these funds to purchase it.

You did not participate in, nor did you influence, the
selection of Indian Stone Corral County Park for funding from the
1988 Bond Act. Nor did you participate in or influence the
selection of the acquisition of your property as an addition to
Indian Stone Corral County Park. The selection of this property
and the subsequent funding source were independent actions of the
county.

ANALYSIS

Conflict of Interest

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 states that
a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate
family or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

File No. A-96-058
Page 3


     (b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

     (e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
[two hundred eighty dollars ($280)] or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to the
public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made.

* * *

Section 82048 of the Act states that "public official" means
"every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency, but does not include judges and court
commissioners in the judicial branch of government." You have
been retired from the Department of Parks and Recreation since
December 30, 1994. Therefore, you are no longer considered a
"public official" subject to the Act's conflict of interest
requirements. Moreover, your facts indicate that no income was
"promised" to you by the county at the time you were a public
official.

Revolving Door

     Officials who have left state service are subject to two
types of restrictions under the Act. Section 87406 of the Act
prohibits specified officials, for one year after leaving state
service, from being paid to communicate with their former agency
in an attempt to influence agency decisions that involve the
making of general rules (such as regulations and legislation), or
to influence a permit, license, contract, or transaction involving
property or goods. Because you retired from the Department of
Parks and Recreation on December 30, 1994, the time period of the
one-year ban expired on December 30, 1995. It no longer applies
to you.

     Sections 87400-87405 of the Act prohibit specified officials
from being paid to appear before their former agency in a

File No. A-96-058
Page 4


proceeding involving specific parties (e.g., a lawsuit,
administrative law judge hearing, or a state contract) in which
the official participated while in state service. The transaction
in question is the sale of your house to the county. In the
course of this transaction, you are not being paid to appear
before the Department of Parks and Recreation in a proceeding in
which you participated while working at the Department. Thus, the
Act's revolving door provisions would not affect the sale of your
property to the county.

     I trust this answers your questions. If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at 916/322-
5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Hyla P. Wagner
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:HPW:ak
